medications.indication_rationale: Effective analgesia/anti-inflammatory for pharyngitis; source gave ibuprofen 400mg stat and alternating with paracetamol. No contraindication reported.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 400mg orally every 6–8 hours with food as needed (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Alternated with ibuprofen for continuous pain control to enable oral rehydration; source advises 1000mg regularly.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000mg orally every 4–6 hours (max 4g/day), alternating with ibuprofen (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Topical anaesthetic for local symptom relief; source used benzydamine 0.15%.
medications.dose_route_frequency: as per product directions (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Clear viral features with low Centor — antibiotics are not indicated and would be an error of commission.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take pain relief ~30 minutes before attempting to drink; sip cold fluids and suck ice chips.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Timing analgesia to the task of drinking maintains hydration and avoids an ED visit for IV fluids.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Prefer cool, bland fluids; avoid acidic drinks like orange juice.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Cold soothes and numbs; acidic drinks irritate inflamed mucosa.

patient_education_points.point: This is a viral throat infection — the cough, runny nose and red eyes point to a virus, so antibiotics won't help. Pain peaks around days 3–5 then settles over about a week.
The key is pain control so you can drink: take the pain relief about half an hour before drinking, then sip cold fluids and ice chips through the day.

Go to the ED if you can't open your mouth fully, your voice becomes muffled, you develop one-sided throat swelling or drooling, or you stop passing urine / feel very lightheaded — these need urgent assessment.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Ability to maintain oral hydration, pain control, fever trajectory, any abscess features.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Cannot resume drinking despite analgesia, trismus/hot-potato voice/unilateral swelling/drooling, or signs of significant dehydration — attend ED.

must_include_items: Identify viral cause and withhold antibiotics (low Centor)
Screen for/safety-net peritonsillar abscess (trismus, hot-potato voice, unilateral swelling, drooling)
Dehydration safety-net (can't drink / not passing urine / lightheaded → ED)
acceptable_alternatives: Paracetamol and/or NSAID both acceptable; benzydamine or lozenges acceptable topical adjuncts
Telehealth or GP follow-up both acceptable
errors_of_omission: No analgesia plan to enable drinking
Failing to identify the viral cause
errors_of_commission: Prescribing antibiotics for clearly viral pharyngitis
Dismissing the dehydration risk in a patient living alone who cannot drink
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires an effective analgesia-to-hydrate plan, no antibiotics, and both an abscess and a dehydration safety-net.
